title: Uncommon Knowledge: Trump Hits Troubled Waters in Mexico Tariff Fight
description: Santa Rosa, Texas, suffered an unfortunate landmark last year: its last sugar mill shut down after half a century, the victim of an ongoing and severe drought. The parched Rio Grande Valley watched a once-staple industry flicker out. Econom
content:
Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum with U.S. President Donald Trump during the draw for the 2026 FIFA Football World in Washington, DC, on December 5...Read More | Jia Haocheng / POOL / AFP via Getty Images
Santa Rosa, Texas, suffered an unfortunate landmark last year: its last sugar mill shut down after half a century, the victim of an ongoing and severe drought. The parched Rio Grande Valley watched a once-staple industry flicker out. Economists at Texas A&M had already forecast nearly a $1 billion hit to the region's economy from 2023 irrigation shortages; by 2024, the mill’s gates were chained.
Yesterday, President Donald Trump waded into that longstanding frustration with a blunt, and understandable, threat: unless Mexico releases 200,000 acre-feet of Rio Grande water by December 31, the U.S. will impose an extra 5 percent tariff on Mexican imports—"IMMEDIATELY." It’s classic Trump rhetoric, and it landed because the pain in South Texas is real. But here’s the twist: under the very same binational regime, Mexico is already propping up the American Southwest by conserving water in Lake Mead—a commitment that keeps the reservoir a couple of feet higher and delays painful cuts for U.S. cities and farms—and in 2024 Washington and Mexico added a new measure to conserve 400,000 acre-feet through 2026, roughly twice the one-off volume Trump is demanding on the Rio Grande.
Mexico is propping up Lake Mead even as Texas runs dry, and sinking trade with Trump tariffs won't change that reality.
The 1944 Water Treaty obliges Mexico to deliver 1.75 million acre-feet to the U.S. from six Rio Grande tributaries over each five-year cycle; Trump says Mexico is behind and demands 200,000 acre-feet now or face tariffs.
On the right and among farmers, the mood is exasperation edged with fury. Texas Farm Bureau state director Brian Jones has called Mexico’s repeated shortfalls "infuriating," warning that planted acres are down by as much as 45 percent in the Valley and that "we risk losing an entire industry" if nothing changes. "Something must be done and done now," he said.
On the left—and among many economists—the alarm more generally is about the tool. For former Biden Treasury official Kimberly Clausing, Trump's tariff push is "the single most self-destructive economic policy move I’ve seen in my lifetime… [and] a very large consumer tax increase."
Diplomats warn of a different risk: linkage. Start tying water to tariffs (and migration, and fentanyl), says former Mexican ambassador Arturo Sarukhán, and "the danger is that all of the agenda… simply freezes." In other words, smash the plumbing and you don’t get more water—you get fewer ways to solve anything.
The treaty, crucially, has two ledgers. The one Trump has put loudly in the headlines is the Rio Grande: 1.75 million acre-feet per five-year cycle from Mexico to the U.S., designed that way because those Mexican tributaries are erratic. Mexico often catches up late, but Texas farmers hate the timing. Trump’s 200,000 acre-feet now-or-tariffs demand represents around 65 billion gallons.
The quieter ledger is the Colorado River: the U.S. owes Mexico 1.5 million acre-feet every single year, and when Lake Mead drops, both countries take shortage cuts under agreements called Minutes. Critically, Mexico can bank conserved water in Lake Mead—and has been doing so. Federal briefings note that Mexican conservation and storage have nudged Mead a couple of feet higher, buying time for American users tied to the reservoir’s shortage tiers. In April 2024, the two governments added Minute 330, under which Mexico will conserve an additional 400,000 acre-feet through 2026 to help prop up Mead—about double the one-time 200,000 Trump demands on the Rio Grande.
None of this excuses shortfalls that leave Texas orchards short; the Santa Rosa mill’s closure is not an abstraction. But it does show the relationship as a two-way system. Treat the Rio Grande dispute as a morality play and tariffs feel satisfying. See the Colorado ledger and the case for smashing trade to make a point looks shakier. The practical route is duller and more useful: use the treaty’s gear-work to make Rio Grande deliveries predictable (the International Water and Boundary Commission is already testing that with new steps to boost reliability and predictability), while keeping the Lake Mead bargain intact. The solution is likely plumbing, not tariffs.
Update, 12/10/25, 10:40 a.m. ET: This article was updated to include comments from former Biden Treasury official Kimberly Clausing

title: Trump Warns of ‘Dark and Sinister Forces’ as Supreme Court Decision Looms
description: President Donald Trump warned that the Supreme Court ruling against him in a key case on his power to apply tariffs would be the "biggest threat in history to United States National Security."
content:
President Donald Trump warned that the Supreme Court ruling against him in a key case on his power to apply tariffs would be the "biggest threat in history to United States National Security."
Trump said tariffs have "greatly enhanced" national security and that they had made the U.S. the "financially strongest" country in the world, adding: "Only dark and sinister forces would want to see that end!!!"
In November, Supreme Court justices heard oral arguments in the case that challenged Trump's executive authority to impose tariffs—his signature economic policy—over the head of Congress.
The court expedited the case after a request from the Trump administration. Justices are yet to make a ruling, one that could upend a foundation stone of Trump's policy agenda.
Lower courts have repeatedly ruled against the administration's authority in imposing broad and sweeping tariffs on an emergency basis.
U.S. President Donald Trump arrives for the 48th Kennedy Center Honors gala at the Kennedy Center in Washington, DC, on December 7, 2025. | Brendan SMIALOWSKI / AFP via Getty Images
Trump has liberally used tariffs since retaking office in January. He sees them as a vital tool of American economic power, one that can protect and rebuild American manufacturing and production sectors by deterring unfair global competition.
The Republican president has also used tariffs to punish U.S. rivals and foes, such as China for its role in the fentanyl trade and Russian trading partners for their role in aiding the Kremlin's war on Ukraine.
But critics of the tariffs—a tax paid by the importer—say they are pushing up inflation and can deprive American firms of raw materials and other products they need.
Moreover, they say Trump has also used tariffs aggressively against allies, weakening vital strategic partners that the U.S. should be helping to strengthen.
And they have prompted trading partners to hit back with their own tariffs on American imports, hurting U.S. exporters.
A potential majority in a ruling against the tariffs would almost certainly bring together the court’s three liberal justices and at least two conservatives.
Justices Amy Coney Barrett and Neil Gorsuch, both Trump appointees, and Chief Justice John Roberts appeared to be the most likely to rule against the president.
The Constitution gives Congress the power to impose tariffs, but Trump has claimed extraordinary power to act without congressional approval by declaring national emergencies under the 1977 International Emergency Economic Powers Act.
At the hearing for oral arguments, Gorsuch signaled he was troubled by the idea that Congress could give away its power over taxes to the president.
"The power to reach into the pockets of the American people is just different and it’s been different since the founding," Gorsuch said, when disputes over taxes helped spark the American Revolution.
Both Barrett and Roberts asked questions indicating at least some unease about how the case should come out, and Justices Brett Kavanaugh, Samuel Alito and Clarence Thomas also could support the administration.
Kavanaugh asked about the 10 percent worldwide tariffs imposed by President Richard Nixon under a predecessor to IEEPA that used very similar language.
Understanding Nixon’s tariffs, which were upheld by an appellate court but never reached the Supreme Court, "is real important to deciding this case correctly," he said.
Barrett and Kavanaugh seized on arguments made by the challengers that the president could order a complete trade embargo but not impose tariffs of even 1 percent under the emergency law.
"Doesn’t it seem like it would make sense, then, that Congress would want the president to use something that was…weaker medicine than completely shutting down trade as leverage to try to get a foreign nation to do something?" Barrett asked.
The president has said that the U.S. is taking in "trillions" of dollars from import tariffs and other investments.
But the actual numbers are far less than that. Treasury Department data shows that revenue from tariffs in the last fiscal year was nearly $195 billion.
In the current fiscal year, tariffs have earned around $31 billion so far.

description: Democrats ‘Laser Focus’ Messaging Is Key For 2026 Midterms: DGA Executive Director
content:
People aligned with the Democrats have mockingly praised a new Republican strategy to put President Donald Trump on the campaign trail ahead of the midterm elections.
Writing on X, Democrats reacted to a The Mom Viewwill put Trump "on the ballot" to encourage voter turnout.
"He’s going to have a fun next year, but we’re going to put him on the campaign trail, too," she said.
The Republican Party recently performed poorly in a series of elections on November 4, including in the New York City mayoral election, in which Democratic socialist Zohran Mamdani defeated independent Andrew Cuomo and Republican Curtis Sliwa. The party will be looking to learn from their mistakes and perform better in future elections.
President Donald Trump speaks during a roundtable on farm subsidies in the Cabinet Room of the White House, Monday, December 8, 2025, in Washington. | (AP Photo/Alex Brandon)
This is not least because Republicans have a slim majority of 219 to 214 in the House of Representatives and hold a 53-47 majority in the Senate. Losing just a few seats in the midterms could make it difficult for the GOP to carry out its agenda during the rest of Trump's term.
Traditionally, the party that does not hold the White House tends to do better in midterm elections. In 2018, Republicans lost the House and the Democrats gained 41 seats.
In response, X user Grant Gregory wrote: "As I Dem I couldn’t encourage this more! Get him out there! Get him on TV! Make the midterms about him. That would be fantastic."
Izzy Gordon, California Governor Gavin Newsom's direct of communications wrote: "A gift to Democrats. PLEASE, SUSIE, GET TRUMP EVERYWHERE!!!!"
Dan Pfeiffer, co-host of Pod Save America and a former adviser to Barack Obama, wrote: "please."
California Democrat Ted Lieu wrote: "THANK YOU for rerunning your 2017-18 strategy of putting Trump on the ballot!! Trump is old and weak, but please make him visit every swing district."
It comes as Trump has been hit with negative polling. A recent poll by The Economistlowest level since he returned to office in January, with 39 percent approving of the job he is doing and 58 percent disapproving, a net approval rating of -19 points. Nevertheless, Trump has claimed he is popular and that polls like these are "fake."
She added: "He's going to campaign like it's 2024 again…He's a difference maker and he's certainly a turnout machine."
"Wiles is likely right that putting Trump back out on the campaign trial will energize low propensity Republican voters; but it will also further energize Democrat voters and probably not change the overall trajectory of the election."

title: Donald Trump Pennsylvania Rally: When Is It and How to Watch
description: President Donald Trump will travel to Pennsylvania on Tuesday to headline a rally in the Pocono Mountains, aiming to bolster his economic message amid persistent public skepticism about his handling of inflation and prices.
content:
President Donald Trump will travel to Pennsylvania on Tuesday to headline a rally in the Pocono Mountains, aiming to bolster his economic message amid persistent public skepticism about his handling of inflation and prices.
The event marks a shift from official White House messaging to a campaign-style setting as Trump seeks to reframe national anxieties about affordability ahead of the 2026 midterms.
“We’re bringing prices way down,” Trump said Monday at the White House. “You can call it ‘affordability’ or anything you want, but the Democrats caused the affordability problem, and we’re the ones that are fixing it."
The visit comes as Republicans recalibrate their economic messaging following poor showings in recent off-year elections. Trump has blamed his predecessor, Democrat Joe Biden, for inflation, but some of his own policies, including sweeping tariffs enacted in April, have drawn criticism for driving up consumer prices.
With affordability now a central issue for both parties, Trump’s rally will serve as a public test of his claims that his administration is curbing inflation through deregulatory moves and targeted price negotiations on drugs and energy.
President Donald Trump arrives for a rally to kick off the July Fourth holiday weekend at the Iowa State Fairgrounds on July 3, 2025. | Scott Olson/Getty Images
Trump will address supporters in a congressional district considered critical to House control next year. Republican Representative Rob Bresnahan won the seat in 2024 by just 1.5 percentage points, and Democrats have already lined up challengers, including Scranton Mayor Paige Cognetti.
The rally will take place at Mount Airy Casino Resort in Monroe County, according to the Pennsylvania Republican Party, and is set to begin at 6 p.m., according to WVIA, a Scranton-based PBS and NPR affiliate.
Monroe County itself flipped from Biden to Trump in 2024, helping to deliver Pennsylvania’s 19 electoral votes and secure Trump's return to the White House. The area, anchored by tourism and affordable housing markets, has seen rising costs in groceries, energy and insurance.
Trump has recently touted changes to federal fuel-efficiency rules and interest-rate pressure on the Federal Reserve as strategies to ease cost burdens. Critics argue such moves could worsen inflation, but the administration believes economic momentum will improve perceptions by mid-2026.
Although the campaign has not posted official livestream details as of Tuesday morning, Trump's speaking events are consistently broadcast live on the White House's official YouTube channel and covered by major cable news channels and select online outlets.
The Mount Airy rally is the first in a series of domestic events planned to showcase Trump’s economic record and policy goals ahead of next year’s election. His administration is expected to hold similar events across other battleground districts throughout the winter and spring.

title: Conventional Wisdom: Warner Bros. Bidding War Edition
description: Netflix announced last Friday that it would acquire Warner Bros. Discovery’s studio and streaming assets for $82.7 billion, only to face a hostile counteroffer from Paramount Skydance on Monday of $108.4 billion for the entire company. The
content:
Netflix announced last Friday that it would acquire Warner Bros. Discovery’s studio and streaming assets for $82.7 billion, only to face a hostile counteroffer from Paramount Skydance on Monday of $108.4 billion for the entire company. The stakes transcend the future of Hollywood with both bidders paying their respects to President Donald Trump beforehand. Netflix co-CEO Ted Sarandos made a November visit to the Oval Office. Meanwhile, the son of world’s-second-richest-man Ralph Ellison, Paramount CEO David Ellison, was spotted in the presidential box at Sunday’s Kennedy Center Honors before unveiling his bid backed by, among others, Jared Kushner’s private equity firm and the sovereign wealth funds of Saudi Arabia, Qatar and the UAE. Whatever happens, Trump has said he’ll “be involved,” which means the corporate dealmaking won’t be about just the numbers.
While the Ellisons spent months telegraphing their inevitable WBD play premised on their MAGA-world credentials making them regulatory shoo-ins, Netflix’s co-CEO wooed in stealth-mode, right up until Trump stunned the Kennedy Center crowd by toasting "my friend Ted." Sarandos didn't just beat the Ellisons in the WBD boardroom; he outflanked them in the only room that matters.
The Oracle founder operated as if his Paramount takeover bought him a permanent regulatory hall pass. But Trump’s Monday morning attack on CBS News proved Ellison broke the cardinal rule: don’t trade on the boss's name before the next check clears. He made a critical error for someone aspiring to be the king of streaming: Loyalty in this White House isn't an asset you own—it's a subscription on auto-renew.
For two years, Wall Street and Hollywood alike called his merger of Discovery with Time Warner a train wreck as debt ballooned and the stock cratered. But chaos is a ladder. He might not have created "the premier media and entertainment company in the world,” but he managed to find the ultimate escape hatch: sparking a bidding war between two sides, both with deep pockets and huge egos.
She had one job. Installed at CBS News as a MAGA heat shield, she spent weeks lecturing the broadcast veterans on how to do their jobs, only to fail the only KPI that matters when Trump declared in all-caps on Truth Social that the Ellisons “ARE NO BETTER THAN THE OLD OWNERSHIP.” But don’t worry: she still gets to keep all her Free Press
She rallied 550 industry luminaries including Pedro Pascal and Viola Davis to warn that a Netflix deal would amount to a "constitutional crisis" (and that was before the Ellisons showed up), but the veteran activist’s protest only highlighted the diminished stature of the movie business. For a community defined by its inflated self-importance, Hollywood royalty has never looked smaller than when it is watching centibillionaires and sovereign wealth funds determine their industry’s fate. The stars used to run this town; now they’re just tenants.
The network finds itself in a bizarre limbo where the $5.8 billion breakup fee Netflix owes WBD if its deal doesn’t go through is more than the cable news giant is worth. Best case? A spinoff into a standalone cable asset like its rival MSNOW, né MSNBC. Worst case? It becomes a political bargaining chip to grease a ten-figure merger. Staffers are praying they don’t suffer the same fate as CBS News, which Shari Redstone had no qualms about sacrificing to get her Paramount deal done. [cue James Earl Jones voice
CW assigns arrows—up, down, or sideways—to the figures and forces shaping current events. The arrows don't predict the future or claim special insight. They capture the prevailing winds of the moment, uncluttered by tribal howling. In an era when partisan media reinforces rather than questions assumptions, CW operates from the center—skeptical of left and right alike, committed to puncturing inflated reputations and recognizing overlooked truths.

title: Don’t Let Voters Cancel Capitalism
description: Across America, capitalism is on trial. Interest in democratic socialism is surging—nowhere more visibly than in New York City, which just elected a democratic socialist mayor, Zohran Mamdani. National figures like Senator Bernie Sanders an
content:
Across America, capitalism is on trial. Interest in democratic socialism is surging—nowhere more visibly than in New York City, which just elected a democratic socialist mayor, Zohran Mamdani. National figures like Senator Bernie Sanders and Representatives Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez have helped normalize the label and its policy agenda with younger voters. Why does this message land so strongly with Gen Z? And is there a way to improve what we have without destroying the economic engine that has built our economy since its earliest days?
When more than half of those under 40 believe our system no longer works for them, they’re not wrong. Our young people see symptoms everywhere of an economic model that has failed to keep pace with the reality facing the vast majority of American families and young people. The middle class has thinned; paychecks have lagged productivity; and union power has waned while rewards at the top exploded with
But before we decide capitalism is the disease, we should be precise about the cure democratic socialism promises—and the results it tends to deliver. Classical socialism vests ownership or control of major industries in the state, aspiring to equitable allocation. In practice, state-directed economies struggle to process information and set prices, producing misallocation, inefficiencies, shortages and stagnation. Venezuela’s recent collapse under price controls and nationalizations is a painful modern example.
Skeptics insist you can’t do good and do well—that “superimposing a social agenda” comes at the expense of financial returns. Real-world experience says otherwise. Done right, companies that employ socially and environmentally responsible
At my company, we invest at scale in everyday systems working families rely on—workforce housing, high performing public-charter schools and community-serving health care facilities—because demand is durable and because serving people well is good business.
Capitalism is a social contract; if the gains feel exclusive, people will reach for a different economic model at the ballot box. Business self-regulation won’t be enough. We need clear, pro-growth rules and incentives that make inclusive capitalism the rational default for both corporations and investors.
For starters we could create certified inclusive enterprise standards “CIES” that demand B-Corp rigor but are also tied to audited outcomes based on inclusive capitalism practices. Companies that meet these standards could benefit from tax breaks, expedited and predictable regulatory treatment, accelerated public procurement and permitting, favorable zoning and entitlements and other incentives. In fact, programs like these already exist in
Capital providers could also get a powerful nudge: treat distributions from certified companies or investments as federally tax-exempt, similar to municipal bonds. States could piggyback with matching exemptions. The result is simple: make it more profitable to invest in businesses that preserve affordability, expand access and measurably improve community outcomes.
Capitalism is a privilege, not an entitlement. It survives only if it creates a fair chance at prosperity for the many, not just the few. A vote for socialism is, at its core, a vote against an economic model that has excluded most Americans. Let’s fix that—by making inclusive capitalism the winning business strategy.

title: Meghan Markle’s Complex Relationship With Her Dad
description: Meghan Markle's father—regularly maligned by her online supporters—has been one of the major enigmas surrounding her childhood.
content:
Meghan Markle attends the Project Healthy Minds World Mental Health Day Gala at Spring Studios, in New York City, on October 9, 2025. | Ilya S. Savenok/Getty Images for Project Healthy Minds
Meghan Markle's father—regularly maligned by her online supporters—has been one of the major enigmas surrounding her childhood.
Meghan and Thomas Markle are now estranged, and the Duchess of Sussex was left calling hospitals over the weekend trying to track him down after a life-or-death leg amputation in the Philippines.
It all throws back into the light the complicated relationship between Meghan and a man who was once going to walk her down the aisle at her royal wedding but is now left telling the media, "I don't want to die estranged."
The duchess used to speak glowingly about her father in posts on her lifestyle blog The Tig, which she closed when she joined the royal family.
Meghan Markle attends the Project Healthy Minds World Mental Health Day Gala in New York City on October 9, 2025. | Ilya S. Savenok/Getty Images for Project Healthy Minds
Prince Harry’s U.K. Threat Assessment Sparks Major Speculation
"I was with my mom during the week and then with my dad on the weekends. And my dad lived alone. He had two adult children who had moved out of his house.
"I was a daddy's girl my whole life and I was with him a lot." She added that: "I do remember also feeling lonely when I was a kid and wanting to have more people around."
The duchess has always maintained that she had a good relationship with her father up until 2018 and the run-up to her wedding to Prince Harry, in Windsor that May.
There was major speculation in the media at the time about whether her father would make the St George's Chapel ceremony. Then, pictures began to emerge in British newspapers showing him getting measured for a suit or reading about his daughter in an internet café.
During the case, a filing described the letter: "The contents and character of the Letter were intrinsically private, personal and sensitive in nature: it concerned the Claimant’s relationship with her father; her constant love for and desire to protect him; the financial support she had given him (contrary to his public claim that she had not); her concerns over the state of his health; the recent breakdown of that relationship; her feelings about that breakdown and the very painful impact of his conduct upon her, in particular his dealings with the press; and her sense of betrayal over his denial of working with the paparazzi."
Before the wedding, Markle kept his lips sealed about his daughter, but once he had been outed, he gave an interview to TMZ. He also experienced two heart attacks before the ceremony, which ruled him out of attending.
Afterward, he began giving regular interviews to the British media and at one stage said he would do one a month until she agreed to speak to him again.
"I've made it quite clear that until I hear from her, until she does speak to me, that I will continue to do this," he told Good Morning Britain in October 2021.
"I will do a show at least a month, if I can get it through, and eventually sooner or later she'll start talking to me. This is way too childish; this is kind of silly, it's time to talk with each other, we're family."
He also challenged Meghan's account of her childhood, given in a 2021 open letter she wrote in support of the Paid Leave for All campaign.
"I grew up on the $4.99 salad bar at Sizzler," Meghan wrote, "it may have cost less back then (to be honest, I can't remember)—but what I do remember was the feeling: I knew how hard my parents worked to afford this because even at five bucks, eating out was something special, and I felt lucky. And as a Girl Scout, when my troop would go to dinner for a big celebration, it was back to that same salad bar or The Old Spaghetti Factory—because that's what those families could afford to do too."
Needless to say, the sudden and total disintegration of the relationship has long puzzled observers.
One fan on X, @QueenRMade1, held up Meghan's mother, Doria Ragland, as an example of a better parent in a December 6: "If you want your child not to be estranged from you, then be a Doria Ragland not a Thomas Markle."
Another, @sussexopulence, wrote on X: "Thomas Markle, willingly took part in his own exploitation, and the ongoing effort to destroy his own daughter, colluding with the Daily Mail and [journalist] Caroline Graham, for over 9 years. Should he need help with his medical bills he should forward them to the Daily Mail. In my opinion."
Do you have a question about King Charles III and Queen Camilla, Prince William and Princess Kate, Meghan Markle and Prince Harry, or their family that you would like our experienced royal correspondents to answer? Email royals@newsweek.com. We'd love to hear from you.

title: Engaged Couple Buy Dream Home—Not Prepared for What They Find in Attic
description: A woman had just moved to a new home with her fiance when she discovered something incredible in the attic.
content:
A woman had just moved to a new home with her fiance when she discovered something incredible in the attic.
Kaitlyn Ostolaza, 23, is from Illinois, and recently bought a house with her fiance in a new state, when her parents came to see their new home and help them decorate. But, during this visit, Ostolaza told Newsweek
Curious, the man fixed a string to the attic door, managed to pull it down, and went up to explore—where he found an old wedding dress, carefully hidden away and packaged to protect it from the elements.
"Because he sent me a text of the box, I always knew that it was a wedding dress based on what he sent to me and the label on the box," Ostolaza said. "It wasn't until weeks later, however, when I had my fiance go into the attic to get the box."
In the footage, viewed more than 2.4 million times, Ostolaza explained in words over the clip: "POV [point of view]: You bought a house with your fiance and find a wedding dress in the attic."
From left: Kaitlyn Ostolaza is seen with the box found in the attic. | TikTok @xkirimi
She showed them bringing the giant box down from the attic, before Ostolaza carefully cut open the cardboard box, revealing a carefully plastic-wrapped blue box.
And inside the blue box was a classic, long-sleeved wedding dress, with an elegant pattern on the collar, train and sleeves. She pulled it out to show how it looked against her body, before giving viewers a close-up of the once-beloved item.
In the caption, she said she was "hoping to use something left behind as my something borrowed for our wedding.
"I just know whoever wore this dress looked amazing in it," she added. "The train is everything."
And while the dress is "beautiful," she admitted it "isn't ultimately my style—but I really want to incorporate it into my wedding day."
"There have been a lot of good ideas left in my comment section about turning it into a reception dress, getting-ready robe," Ostolaza said, "or there is an artist on the platform who creates pieces using wedding dresses that might be a cute way to display [it] in the home."
The wedding dress is displayed in the box, left, and held up by a person. | TikTok @xkirimi
And Ostolaza isn't finished exploring her new house, either, as she revealed: "In the same attic above the garage, there were some pool floaties, [and] there is another attic above the home that we have yet to explore."
TikTok users loved the story, with one writing: "That looks exactly like my moms wedding dress. I transformed it into mine for my wedding by cutting off the sleeves and altering it."
"Whoever dry cleaned and preserved that dress did an amazing job," another posted, while a third commented they would "wear that in a HEARTBEAT."
"This is one of those things that was meant to happen," one viewer said. "Someone you don’t even know gave you a wonderful gift! Use it! You may have a granddaughter who will want to use it."
Others choose to frame it, donate it to charity, sell it on, or repurpose it into something new.
Some commenters worried that the dress could belong to whoever owned the house before the people Ostolaza bought it from, with some urging her to try to track them down.
Others argued that the dress had clearly been there for years, if not decades, and if it didn't belong to the previous homeowners, it would be extremely difficult to track them down.

title: Prince Harry Threat Level Mystery Raises Extraordinary Possibility
description: Prince Harry is being given a new threat assessment by the British Government for the first time since quitting palace life—in a puzzling move that brings his relationship with the U.K. back into the light.
content:
Prince Harry is being given a new threat assessment by the British Government for the first time since quitting palace life—in a puzzling move that brings his relationship with the U.K. back into the light.
The Duke of Sussex had his police protection team stripped from him in 2020 after announcing his move to North America. He has been fighting to get the armed officers back ever since, including through the U.K. courts.
And he has been pushing for a particular risk assessment, which he hopes will deliver a threat level high enough that Government officials will consider him to need a police team again during trips to Britain.
Meghan Markle and Prince Harry attend a National Service of Thanksgiving to celebrate the Platinum Jubilee of Queen Elizabeth II at St Paul's Cathedra...Read More | Max Mumby/Indigo/Getty Images
Harry sued the government to try to force them to give him a Risk Management Board [RMB] assessment and lost—but they are bizarrely now giving him one anyway, raising questions about why officials would suddenly change course.
Some in the media have argued it must be a product of warming relations with King Charles III, but the Home Office always insisted it was never about the royal rift. That raises another hypothetical possibility, which is far more simple.
Officials appeared to say Harry's risk assessments were stopped simply because he left Britain. So one thing that could trigger a new risk assessment would be if Harry was planning to move back.
Some, though, may think that stretches the boundaries of believability given Harry's enviable life in sunny California. Either way, the assessment should be concluded next month, meaning royal watchers may not have long to wait to find out.
Prince Harry’s U.K. Threat Assessment Sparks Major Speculation
Decisions on who gets police protection are made by RAVEC, a committee of the U.K. Home Office which also contains senior police and royal household aides.
Sir Richard Mottram was its chair in 2020 when the decision was made to strip Harry of his team. Mottram gave a witness statement to the High Court suggesting it was Harry's decision to move abroad that triggered the end of his risk assessments.
This evidence was summarized by Judge Sir Peter Lane in 2024: "As regards RMB assessments, the witness statement explains that one consequence in the change of [Harry's] role and his decision to live mainly abroad was that the assessment of risk that the RMB had conducted in respect of the claimant in April 2019 had been overtaken by events. Moreover, the methodology underpinning the assessments of the RMB [redacted text].
"Sir Richard Mottram ultimately saw no merit, therefore, in conducting a formal RMB process for [Harry]. The decision to consider the case for [redacted text] was, in Sir Richard’s view, a flexible and tailored approach, which better matched [Harry's] revised circumstances."
RAVEC's decision was never presented in court as a product of Harry not being at risk but rather the risk assessment process being designed for U.K. residents. Instead, RAVEC created a new system where they would assess whether to give Harry police protection on a case-by-case basis for each visit.
The judgment in a later Court of Appeal case, handed down in May, read: "RAVEC had not considered commissioning a risk analysis from the Risk Management Board after February 2020, in essence because that was, in the opinion of the relevant experts, unnecessary."
Sir Richard felt “it made much more sense to assess the threat and risk issues" from Harry's U.K. trips "by reference to their particular context; rather than conduct [a risk analysis] for a principal who was no longer based in Great Britain and whose activities when he was present were not likely to be regular."
It all makes the current decision to back down and conduct a RMB assessment a peculiar one, particularly given the Home Office won the case.
Ignoring everything known about Harry as a person, a clean and simple explanation would be that the prince returning to Britain would likely trigger a new RMB assessment.
That is very hard to marry though with everything known about Harry, who appears on the face of it to be enjoying life in America even if the current president is not exactly his cup of tea.
Seward cited a recent drama over Prince Harry's visa in light of his revelations in his book Sparetaken recreational drugs. Right-wing think tank The Heritage Foundation appealed for Donald Trump to strip Harry of his visa, though the president has not taken them up on the request.
"There's then also the possibility that Harry might want his children educated here," Seward said.
There are other theories too. Security expert Alex Bomberg, the chief executive of private security firm Intelligent Protection International, told Newsweek
However, his primary theory was the police may have picked up new information about a threat to Harry.
One more orthodox argument in the British press is that Harry simply managed to sweet talk his way into getting what he wanted through a letter to British Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood, who only recently came into post in September, replacing Yvette Cooper.
There is an argument for saying both Labour Party politicians may have been more favorable to Harry's cause than the Conservative Party predecessors in post during Harry and Meghan Markle's early years in America.
After all, Cooper and Mahmood both signed a 2019 letter of solidarity in defense of Meghan, which did not bear the names of their Tory predecessors, Suella Braverman, Priti Patel, James Cleverly and Grant Shapps.
“On occasions, stories and headlines have represented an invasion of your privacy and have sought to cast aspersions about your character, without any good reason as far as we can see.
“Even more concerning still, we are calling out what can only be described as outdated, colonial undertones to some of these stories.”
It concluded: "You have our assurances that we stand with you in solidarity on this. We will use the means at our disposal to ensure that our press accept your right to privacy and show respect, and that their stories reflect the truth."
Of course, there remains Harry's perspective that his father had been standing in the way of him getting an RMB assessment, as he told the BBC in May: "I've never asked him to intervene—I've asked him to step out of the way and let the experts do their job."
And he argued that he was denied police protection in an effort to stop him leaving: "This, at the heart of it, is a family dispute. And it makes me really, really sad that we're sitting here today, five years later, where a decision that was made most likely, in fact I know, to keep us under the roof."
From this perspective, it might be argued that improving relations with Charles could give rise to a softening of the hard line stance at the Home Office, though that would fly in the face of the sworn testimony of officials during Harry's lawsuits.
Whatever the cause, the stakes are high for Harry who believes it is not safe for him to bring Meghan and his children, Prince Archie and Princess Lilibet, back to Britain without police protection.
Do you have a question about King Charles III and Queen Camilla, Prince William and Princess Kate, Meghan Markle and Prince Harry, or their family that you would like our experienced royal correspondents to answer? Email royals@newsweek.com. We'd love to hear from you.

title: It’s Time To Talk About Cynthia Erivo and Misogynoir
description: A short red-carpet clip has ignited widespread outrage online after a reporter repeatedly described actor Cynthia Erivo as "tough" and "strong" in an uncomfortable exchange, prompting a wider conversation about misogynoir—misogyny against B
content:
During the interview, the presenter pressed Erivo on being tough, returning to the label even as the actor pushed back. The pair had been discussing Erivo's impressive stunt work during _Wicked: For Good _where her character, Elphaba, flies on a broomstick.
Erivo gently reframed the moment: "I think I'm strong, I think I'm vulnerable, I think I'm a myriad of things."
Still, the interviewer continued: "A protector too. You're tough. You're tough. You're tough; a tough person. You're strong."
Erivo responded with grace: "Well, I'm strong but I think I can also be protected, too. I think we can be both. I think it's OK for us to be strong, protected and protective." Newsweek Cynthia Erivo's publicists for comment via email.
Cynthia Erivo watches during SBIFF's Kirk Douglas Award Honoring Cynthia Erivo at The Ritz Carlton Bacara on December 4, 2025 in Santa Barbara, Califo...Read More | Rebecca Sapp / Contributor/Getty Images Entertainment
The interview followed a recent viral moment where Erivo shielded her WickedAriana Grande after a fan rushed at her on a red carpet. Though the incident was brief, internet users quickly turned Erivo into a meme—many of which leaned on masculinizing tropes.
The jokes—often performed by men—cast Erivo as physically dominant or hypermasculine, despite her being of similar stature to Grande. What many saw as a touching moment of instinctive friendship became a spectacle that played directly into stereotypes historically used to dehumanize Black women.
Morin Abraham, 29, a Toronto-based film content creator known as The Film Diva (Instagram, amassing over 840,000 views—and even a like from Ariana Grande herself.
"There was no reason for the interviewer to label Erivo as ‘tough’, ‘strong’ or a ‘protector.’ Cynthia gave a great response, explaining how she is and can be both protective and protected."
In her video, Abraham said that, while some parody sketches were harmless, many crossed a line—targeting Erivo with "underlying tones" that made her uncomfortable.
Abraham questioned why the jokes frequently came from men, why Erivo was being masculinized, and why audiences were responding with overwhelming enthusiasm.
Abraham said the sheer volume of engagement on these harsher videos left her uneasy, wondering whether she was "too woke," before concluding she likely wasn’t.
Zanetta Miller is owner of Niche PR Boutique, whose previous academic research focused on media representations of Black women—particularly within hip-hop culture. She is currently completing a dissertation on female leaders and gender dynamics in organizational spaces. Miller told Newsweek
"Black women, like those in organizational workspaces and media, are uniquely targeted with stereotyped and biased tropes or controlling images that bleed into online spaces of discourse. As a result, Black women are shaped by these narratives online and must fight against them to maintain their unique identity," Miller said.
She added that misogynoir often appears through the criticism and policing of Black women’s communication, including tone. Confidence may be reframed as attitude; leadership as aggression. Miller noted that Black women face stereotypes tied both to sexism and racism—a dual-burden that American author bell hooks described as "double jeopardy."
She highlighted harmful, enduring tropes identified by Patricia Hill Collins, including: the Mammy (caretaker); Welfare Queen (a lazy woman exploiting social systems); Matriarch (strong Black woman); Jezebel (hypersexualized caricature); and Sapphire (angry Black woman). These are images that have long shaped media portrayals of Black women and influenced how audiences interpret their behavior.
Miller added that digital spaces amplify these biases; she said that online discourse filters reality through cultural narratives. For Black women, this often means their identities are obscured by preexisting stereotypes, making it harder for them to be seen in their full humanity.
Regarding Erivo specifically, Miller said dark-skinned Black women routinely experience disproportionate backlash due to colorism, especially when occupying highly visible cultural roles. By contrast, she noted, white women like Grande are often met with empathy without the racialized scrutiny.
To prevent further harm, Miller said that Hollywood and media culture must dismantle white and male-centered narratives that limit Black women’s representation, integrate Black feminist scholarship—such as the writings of Collins and hooks—into media training, and hire Black women into leadership roles where they can help reshape industry norms.
Abraham emphasized why the language used in the interview matters: "To those on the outside, these labels may sound positive, but they often operate as constraints denying Black women the full emotional range routinely granted to others.
"When a presenter repeatedly leans on these tropes, it reinforces a narrative that Black women are inherently resilient and therefore unworthy of softness, nuance, or care, which is not true."
International pop star SZA wrote: "Ir not bugging and no one is too woke! It’s CLASSIC Misogynoir! NOTHING ELSE!!! can’t believe it’s openly a thing in 2025. everyone’s gonna have cognitive dissonance 2 years later like 'remember when everyone attacked Cynthia for being black bald and nurturing?…that was crazy'..it could jus[t] stop now lol [laugh out loud]."
Fans also voiced their frustration: "They won't ask man these questions," said Aaron.
Another wrote: "Like ha ha it's sooo funny that Ariana got grabbed by a stranger which triggered her PTSD and Cynthia stepped in to protect her. Why are people bullying them for having a close friendship? Why are people masculinizing Cynthia just for being a good friend to Ari?"
"Yeah I agree 100%!! these ‘jokes’ are getting out of hand now," posted Sydney.
"Everywhere you go, there are people desperate to make headlines or escape failure, but here you can clearly see Cynthia's true nature, and we love her," added Edward.

title: Dead ‘Stranger Things’ Characters Fans Believe Could Return in Finale
description: ‘Survivor’ 49 Finalists Love Internet’s Reaction to Trump Address Interrupting Finale
content:
Despite the show’s creators, and even former cast members, hinting that some deaths are final, fans remain deeply invested in one question that has been circulating online for months: Which beloved character could be revived next?
That speculation is part of what has fueled massive engagement for the fifth and final season of one of Netflix's biggest ever shows. The first four episodes of Stranger Things
Fan theories—particularly around the Upside Down's lingering mysteries—have since flooded social media. A popular point of inspiration has been the surprise reappearance of Max Mayfield’s consciousness, trapped in Vecna’s realm while her body lies in a coma in Hawkins General Hospital. David Harbour's Jim Hopper was also found alive in Stranger Things
Now, hopeful viewers are combing through every scene, trailer, and Dungeons & Dragons clue for ways their long-gone favorites might follow in their footsteps. Indeed, the fantasy role-playing game gained cult status with kids in the 70s and 80s, becoming a source inspiration for Matt and Ross Duffer in building the Stranger Things
Eddie Munson’s death in season 4 remains one of the series’ most emotional moments.
The metalhead hero and leader of the "Hellfire" D&D club died in Dustin’s arms after fighting off a swarm of demo-bats. His body, however, was never retrieved, and townsfolk continued to blame him for the murder of Chrissy Cunningham and the spooky events that followed in the once sleepy Midwest town.
This loose end has fueled a wave of fan theories online, including one rooted in Dungeons & Dragons lore.
He told viewers that his idea is linked to Dungeons & Dragons, namely the “Kas Theory,” which draws parallels between Eddie and Kas the Bloody-Handed, a vampire lieutenant of Vecna in the table-top game. According to the theory, Eddie—like Kas—could rise again, potentially under Vecna’s control, before turning on his master, killing him and saving his friends.
The creator also spotted a striking loophole in the series' worldbuilding.
He said that demo-bat bites are not fatal, despite being able to seriously wound people. He finds it hard to fathom how Eddie lost his life to the bats in season 4 considering this detail.
Another theory hinges on Eleven’s powers. In season 4, she revived Max despite her heart stopping for over a minute. Could she do the same for Eddie?
A third possibility comes from a season 4 moment where Nancy Wheeler realizes that time is frozen in the Upside Down on November 5, 1983—the same day Will Byers first went missing in season 1.
Perhaps Eddie, having died within the Upside Down, might somehow be reset to his pre-death self, due to the dimension’s time-freeze.
From left: Joseph Quinn attends Netflix's "Stranger Things" SAG event at Netflix Tudum Theater on November 13, 2022 in Los Angeles, California; and Da...Read More
Billy Hargrove, Max’s volatile stepbrother, was violently killed by the Mind Flayer in season 3 after a rare moment of redemption. His sacrificial death, though final, made him one of the show’s most layered losses.
While Billy has not appeared physically since, bar a few flashbacks, fans see his return as possible through Vecna’s earlier manipulation.
The villain has a history of weaponizing memories and fears against his victims, and Billy’s traumatic presence still lingers in Max’s ongoing storyline. Fans have suggested Billy could resurface in new scenes as a twisted echo, potentially haunting Max again as she lies unconscious in her hospital bed, with her mind tethered to Vecna’s domain.
The first four episodes of the final season of Stranger Things are available to watch. | Netflix
They drew on similarities in how both the "cloned" Billy and Vecna hate the heat and speak warmly to Max, despite them not being on good terms before Billy's car accident that led to him crossing paths with the Upside Down.
Sean Astin’s kindhearted Bob Newby became a fan favorite in Season 2, as Joyce Byers’ sweet, tech-savvy boyfriend who had been pining after her since high school. His heroic death—torn apart by Demodogs while helping others escape from Hawkins Lab—cemented him as a tragic figure in the show’s history.
Given that Bob died in the real world, and outside the temporal distortions of the Upside Down, most fans do not see a full resurrection in the cards. But his legacy remains strong, and some viewers speculate he could reappear in flashbacks.
While exploring the elaborate set, visitors came upon a dummy of Barb Holland’s body covered in inky black vines.
Barb’s death in season 1 was the show’s first major casualty and became the subject of the viral #JusticeForBarb movement, after the town—and the show itself—seemed to move on from her.
This prompted the Duffer brothers to create a storyline in season 2 where Nancy Wheeler and Jonathan Byers team up to get those responsible for the death punished.
“All I know is that my body shows up and I have no idea what the context is. I wasn’t a part of that decision, so I’ll be just as surprised as you are," she said while being interviewed at the season's premier.
While it is unlikely Barb will be revived, the reappearance of her corpse may serve as a final chapter for her arc—especially for Nancy Wheeler, who has long blamed herself for Barb’s tragic fate.
Chrissy Cunningham, killed by Vecna in a brutal season 4 opener, and Fred Benson, another teen victim who held a dark secret, have also drawn fan attention.
Their deaths—bloody and traumatic—helped establish Vecna’s power and horror, but their memory could serve a purpose. Like Billy, Chrissy and Fred might return not in body, but as manifestations of fear or guilt, especially for characters like Lucas and Nancy who witnessed their final moments.
But the looming fear for many fans is not who might come back, it is who might die next.
Chief among their concerns is of course Steve Harrington, played by actor and musician, Joe Keery.
Since season 1, Steve has evolved from detestable jock to one of the series’ most beloved and protective figures. The Duffer brothers have previously admitted that the character was originally meant to die, but actor Joe Keery’s charisma and fan response led them to rewrite his fate.
That history now has fans bracing for what feels like a potential full-circle ending, considering the continuation of the love triangle between Steve, Jonathan and Nancy.
With Stranger Thingsnearing its final hours, death remains a constant presence, both in Hawkins and in the imaginations of fans. The show has made a tradition of killing off supporting characters—Barb, Bob, Billy, Eddie—while sparing its central crew. Whether that pattern continues or a fan-favorite like Steve finally pays the price could define how the series is remembered long after it ends.

title: Sydney Sweeney Snubbed Twice in One Week After Controversial Year
description: Sweeney faced an onslaught of criticism over the summer for her American Eagle jean ad after it was tied to eugenics. For months, the 28-year-old stayed silent on the issue, something she recently said helped fuel the divide, and recently,
content:
Sweeney faced an onslaught of criticism over the summer for her American Eagle jean ad after it was tied to eugenics. For months, the 28-year-old stayed silent on the issue, something she recently said helped fuel the divide, and recently, Sweeney’s politics have been put into the spotlight.
A registered Republican, critics labeled her newest haircut the “MAGA bob,” and Fox News has embraced Sweeney as a means of trying to attract other women to the GOP.
Monday’s Golden Globe nomination left Sweeney off the list. Her absence was noticeable, and the star was included in notable roundups of actors snubbed by the Golden Globes. The snub came just days after she was left off the list of nominees for a Critics Choice award.
Lindsey Bahr, a film writer for the Associated Press, wrote that the Globes should have wanted a huge star like Sweeney nominated for an award.
“It flopped at the box office, but in the most cynical sense, you’d think the show would want Sweeney, who seemingly can’t help going viral, gracing the red carpet,” Bahr wrote.
Sweeney’s jean ad for American Eagle, which was released at the end of July, sparked debate over whether it was racially charged with speculation the use of the word “jeans” was a play on the word “genes.” Critics saw it as sending the message that Sweeney, and people who look like her, are genetically superior.
"Anyone who knows me knows that I’m always trying to bring people together. I’m against hate and divisiveness," Sweeney said.
Actress Sydney Sweeney attends the 16th Governors Awards at the Ray Dolby Ballroom at Ovation Hollywood in Los Angeles on November 16. (Photo by VALER...Read More
Despite not getting any love for her performance from the Golden Globes, Sweeney and the rest of the cast got a standing ovation for the film at the Toronto Film Festival in September. And, she wasn’t the only apparent snub at the Golden Globes, so, the actual impact of her controversial year has yet to be seen.

title: ‘The Boys’ Season 5
description: Prime Video has dropped the thrilling first trailer, and things are looking dire for Butcher and his band of anti-Supe crusaders.
content:
It’s the last ever season of "The Boys," and everyone’s going out with a bang.
Prime Video has dropped the thrilling first trailer, and things are looking dire for Butcher and his band of anti-Supe crusaders.
According to the streamer’s official summary, “It’s Homelander’s world, completely subject to his erratic, egomaniacal whims. Hughie, Mother’s Milk, and Frenchie are imprisoned in a 'Freedom Camp.' Annie struggles to mount a resistance against the overwhelming Supe force. Kimiko is nowhere to be found.”
The summary continues: “But when Butcher reappears, ready and willing to use a virus that will wipe all Supes off the map, he sets in motion a chain of events that will forever change the world and everyone in it. It’s the climax, people.”
“The Boys” Season 5 releases Wednesday, April 8th, 2026, on Prime Video. It premieres with its first two episodes, with new episodes releasing each week on Wednesday until the finale on Wednesday, May 20th, 2026.
“The Boys” Season 5 is a Prime Video exclusive, so it requires a subscription to Prime in order to watch.
Internment camps, SWAT shootouts, and Homelander defrosting Soldier Boy in preparation for a devastating team-up - “The Boys” is looking as action-packed as ever.
And who is that unseen figure Homelander is punching relentlessly? Fans think it’s his increasingly powerful son, Ryan. Watch the full trailer below.
All the key cast for “The Boys” reprise their roles, including Karl Urban (Butcher), Jack Quaid (Hughie), and Antony Starr (Homelander).
"Supernatural" actors Jared Padalecki and Misha Collins join in new roles, while Claudia Doumit (Victoria Neuman) and Jeffrey Dean Morgan (Joe Kessler) are the biggest departures. Here’s the full cast.

title: Federal Reserve Teases Major Changes to Check Services
description: The Federal Reserve is asking the public to weigh in on the future of its check-processing operations, signaling that significant cutbacks may be on the table as the use of paper checks in the United States continues to decline.
content:
The Federal Reserve is asking the public to weigh in on the future of its check-processing operations, signaling that significant cutbacks may be on the table as the use of paper checks in the United States continues to decline.
The Federal Reserve Board last week opened a 90-day comment period to help it determine how much check processing support banks and credit unions still need. Reserve banks currently collect and process checks for financial institutions in exchange for a fee, but the central bank says maintaining those services at current levels would require major investment in aging infrastructure.
According to the Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta, checks have rapidly lost ground as a payment method. In 2020, about 19 percent of household bills were paid by check; by 2024, that figure had fallen to just 7 percent.
“Over time, check use has steadily declined, digital payment methods have grown in availability and use, and check fraud has risen,” the Fed said in a statement. With that shift, officials are unsure whether upgrading the system is justified, prompting the new call for public input.
In its notice, the Fed outlined a range of possible changes, including “significantly reducing check services, or alternatively, substantially winding them down.” To help determine which direction to take, the Board said it is seeking feedback to “help the Board understand stakeholder needs and balance tradeoffs.”
Who Is William DeFoor? 26-Year-Old Accused of Damaging JD Vance’s Home
The move to gather public opinion was approved in a six-to-one vote. The sole dissent came from Michelle Bowman, the Fed’s vice chair for supervision. Bowman warned that the request for public comment itself seemed to hint at an outcome she could not support.
“I cannot support the Request for Information and Comment on the Future of the Federal Reserve Banks’ Check Services,” Bowman said. “The Check Services RFI seems to favor the discontinuation of check services by reserve banks, even while checks remain an important payment mechanism.”
The Fed’s review comes after the federal government stopped using paper checks earlier this year. As of September 30, the Social Security Administration no longer mails paper benefits, shifting to electronic payments. That change was mandated under an executive order signed by President Donald Trump in March.
The comment period is open for 90 days after publication in the Federal Register.

title: Why Flags Are Half-Staff in Two States Today
description: The Stars and Stripes will fly at half-staff across two states in America today after a nationwide alert over the weekend to honor those killed in the Pearl Harbor bombings.
content:
The Stars and Stripes will fly at half-staff across two states in America today after a nationwide alert over the weekend to honor those killed in the Pearl Harbor bombings.
Flags in the United States are lowered to half-staff as a traditional sign of mourning or respect, typically following the death of notable public figures, officials, or as part of a national day of remembrance.
File image: The American flag at half mast in memory of late U.S. President George H. W. Bush before the game between the Buffalo Bills and the New Yo...Read More
Today, the state flags of Ohio and Wyoming are flown at half-staff in honor of individuals recognized for their service and impact on their communities, reflecting the continued importance placed on public service and civic contribution in American life.
This order honors Lieutenant James “Bryan” White of the Madison County Sheriff’s Office, who passed away after a career spanning the Clark County Sheriff’s Office and BCI, concluding with his work in the investigations division of the Madison County Sheriff’s Office.
In Wyoming, Republican Governor Mark Gordon ordered state and U.S. flags lowered through sundown on the date of interment (which remains to be announced) for former State Superintendent of Public Instruction Judy Catchpole.
Serving from 1995 to 2003, Catchpole led substantial reforms in Wyoming's education system, including updates to academic standards, student assessments, teacher certification, and charter school policy.
In Ohio, flags will be restored to full staff following the one-day observance on December 9, 2025. The specific duration for flag-lowering in Wyoming will be determined based on the date of Judy Catchpole’s interment, to be provided by her family.

title: Flood Warning Issued as Excessive Rainfall Breaks River Record
description: A strong atmospheric river is pummeling the Pacific Northwest with significant rainfall, surging water levels at the Yakima River at Easton in Washington state beyond 57 feet on Tuesday morning.
content:
A strong atmospheric river is pummeling the Pacific Northwest with significant rainfall, surging water levels at the Yakima River at Easton in Washington state beyond 57 feet on Tuesday morning.
The record river levels come as National Weather Service (NWS) meteorologists have issued a slew of flood-related warnings for the Pacific Northwest. The strong storm arrived on Monday, only days after a similar yet weaker system saturated the region over the weekend. Storm total rainfall will surpass a foot in some areas, with the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) National Water Center predicting 14 river locations in Washington state to enter major flood stage before the storm moves out of the region.
A stock image shows flooding in Washington state. At least 14 river locations are expected to reach major flood stage because of an ongoing atmospheri...Read More | dschreiber29/Getty Images
Atmospheric rivers are a "long, narrow region in the atmosphere—like rivers in the sky—that transport most of the water vapor outside of the tropics," according to the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.
The storms brought by atmospheric rivers are known for their heavy snow, heavy rain and strong winds. They more commonly affect the West Coast, particularly during the winter months. Although the storms can bring beneficial snow that helps supplement reservoirs throughout the summer dry season, they can also trigger deadly flooding, mudslides and widespread power outages.
Rain is still pummeling the Pacific Northwest. On Tuesday morning, the Yakima River at Easton reached 57.01 feet, an all-time record for the river. Before this storm, the historic record for water levels at the Yakima River at Easton was when it reached 55.28 feet in 1990.
The river has since fallen to 53.25 feet, but meteorologists anticipate it to crest at 58.02 feet by Tuesday afternoon. Clark said it would take a few days for the river to return to normal levels as long as more rain doesn't inundate the area.
Who Is William DeFoor? 26-Year-Old Accused of Damaging JD Vance’s Home
NWS offices in Washington and Oregon have issued numerous flood warnings related to the storm.
"A prolonged atmospheric river will continue impacting the Pacific Northwest with heavy rainfall which has lead to areas of urban and river flooding along with gusty winds into Thursday," the NWS said in a Tuesday forecast.
In addition to heavy rain, strong winds will also pose dangers across the region, threatening power outages and making for difficult travel.

title: Foster Puppy Struggles As Siblings Adopted, Then Another Dog Mom Steps In
description: A tiny, gray rescue puppy was struggling to get healthy in his foster home until an unexpected intervention changed everything.
content:
A tiny, gray rescue puppy was struggling to get healthy in his foster home until an unexpected intervention changed everything.
Lori Comer is a longtime foster for Best Friends Animal Society in Northwest Arkansas. The society is a national animal welfare organization working to make every animal shelter in the U.S. a no-kill one.
Part of that remit means saving every healthy and treatable pet and requires the help of people like Comer. In September, she took in a litter of nine puppies. Among them was a dog who would later be christened Tusche. From the day they met, she knew he was special.
"My first impression of Tusche was just how incredibly tiny he was," Comer told Newsweekthe puppy room, he immediately stood out. Compared to his siblings, Tusche was this tiny, delicate bundle and that smallness was the very first thing that caught my heart."
Caring for puppies that young is no easy task, but Tusche’s size made it particularly challenging for Comer and those at Best Friends who provided support throughout the process.
"Tusche needed intensive hands-on support from the very beginning. At first, I had to make sure he could nurse properly, but by the next morning, he was struggling so much that I kept him warm with heated washcloths until I could get him to the Best Friends clinic," Comer said.
Once Tusche was stabilized, he was placed with a foster who provided round-the-clock specialized feedings. He was eventually returned to Comer at 1 week old and still in need of special care.
"I closely monitored his nursing and supplemented him with frequent bottle feedings to ensure he continued to gain strength," Comer said. "I also placed additional heat sources in the room as well because, since he was so small, his little body couldn’t regulate enough to stay warm."
Tusche the puppy is petted, left, and snuggles with other pups. | Best Friends Animal Society/A puppy who has found a family.
While his siblings grew big and strong in the weeks that followed, Tusche struggled to keep up, needing lots of extra help from Comer and Best Friends in Northwest Arkansas. At 8 weeks old, the rest of the litter got adopted, but Tusche stayed behind, not yet healthy enough to leave Comer’s care.
"Tusche was still so tiny—only about 2 pounds—and not ready to move on like his siblings," Comer said. Then, in October, she got another call from Best Friends asking if she would consider fostering a dog called Donna and her 11 puppies, who were later christened the Dynamos.
Best Friends had a unique idea: if Comer could take the new mom and litter, then she could try integrating Tusche into the litter to help him get healthier. While not a standard practice, Best Friends veterinarians evaluated the situation, took all health precautions, and determined it would be safe to try, given both litters had been born in foster homes.
"Our team of lifesaving specialists and veterinarians at the Best Friends Pet Resource Center in Northwest Arkansas evaluated Tusche’s health and the steps we had taken so far," Castle said. "They also evaluated the health of Donna and her litter to ensure introducing Tusche would be safe for all involved. The team determined that, if Tusche could be integrated into Donna’s litter, that would give him the best chance of getting healthy."
The hope was that Donna and her puppies could help replicate that experience for Tusche while getting well enough to survive on his own and get adopted, something Castle says is "the ultimate goal for every dog and cat in our care."
Comer agreed and, on their very first night when the entire litter woke up around 2 a.m., took the opportunity to place Tusche among them, rubbing him along Donna’s body while she continued to nurse the pups. It was a tactic recommended by Best Friends and it worked.
"She just looked at him, smelled him, and then went back to what she was doing," Comer said. She placed Tusche in among the puppies and before long, they had accepted him as one of their own.
"Despite being almost twice their age, Tusche was still a little bit smaller than the Dynamos litter," Comer said. "It was truly heartwarming to see him fit right in and to witness the surrogate mama's acceptance and nurturing behavior toward him."
From those first tentative steps, Tusche has now taken significant strides in both his growth and personality, with Comer saying, "you'd never know he’s not one of them."
"Being integrated with the surrogate litter tremendously helped him develop social skills such as learning to play," she said. "Being with them also helped him transition to solid food since the surrogate litter was ready to begin the weaning process."
Tusche is now finally ready for adoption. Though Comer will be sad to say goodbye, she can’t wait until the day he finds his way to a forever home of his own. "To see him grow and flourish has warmed my heart," she said. "He’s a wonderful little man, and he’s gonna give someone a run for their money!"
